mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the higher thought processes (G, G.1) including reasoning, concept formation, problem-solving, and deliberation, with sustained treatment of conceptual learning and concept formation (D) through associationism, computationalism, and Platonism/Aristotelianism. Also addresses meaning (G.3) through the language-of-thought hypothesis and inner speech.

Covers the mental phenomena underlying all learning and thinking, including memory, thought, and psychological stages relevant to A and G.1; treatment of conscious and unconscious processes connects to major issues in learning theories (A.2) and the higher thought processes (G, G.1).
Covers learning theories and teaching methods substantively (A, A.1), and addresses major issues such as the roles of motivation, intelligence, and socioeconomic factors in learning (A.2).

Directly and comprehensively covers E (retention and forgetting: encoding, storage, retrieval, interference, decay) and F (abnormalities of memory: amnesia, dementia, PTSD-related disruption), including the structural model distinguishing sensory, short-term, and long-term memory stores.

Directly covers the entire outline division: modern learning theories including classical/operant conditioning and observational learning (A, A.1, A.2), habituation and sensitization addressing major theoretical issues (A.2), transfer across learning domains (A.3), psychomotor learning (B), perceptual learning (C), and conceptual/complex learning forms (D).
Covers cognitive behavior including learning, memory, and decision-making as a domain (A, A.2), and addresses the higher thought processes through deliberation and reasoning (G).

Literacy is directly related to the role of language in higher thought processes (G.2) and to conceptual learning and concept formation (D), as reading comprehension involves both symbolic processing and concept acquisition.
Covers transmission of meaning through coded messages (G.3) and the role of verbal and non-verbal language in human communication (G.2).

Directly addresses the role of language in cognition (G.2), including acquisition, semantics, and the connections between linguistic structure and thought via Broca/Wernicke areas; covers meaning (G.3) through semantic and philosophical treatment.

Directly covers the higher thought processes including deductive, inductive, abductive, and analogical reasoning (G, G.1), and addresses major theoretical issues in cognition such as biases, fallacies, and the neural basis of reasoning (A.2).
Epistemology addresses the nature of knowledge and the sources of belief including perception, memory, and reason (G.1, A.2), and treats meaning, justification, and truth as central concepts (G.3).

As the parent discipline, psychology covers all major learning theories including behaviorism, cognitive, and social schools (A, A.1, A.2), the higher thought processes via cognitive psychology (G, G.1), and memory as a core research area (E).

Freud psychoanalytic theory contributed associationist and unconscious-process accounts relevant to modern learning theories (A.1, A.2), and his work on the unconscious and dream interpretation bears on the psychology of higher thought processes (G.1).
Covers cognitive gains in abstract and hypothetical reasoning and metacognition during adolescence (G.1), which constitute major developmental issues in learning theory (A.2).

Child developmental stages involve progressive cognitive maturation central to major issues in learning theories (A.2), including motor skill acquisition (B) and concept formation across early, middle, and late childhood (D).

Knowledge covers propositional and practical knowing, its sources (perception, memory, inference), and its philosophical analysis (G.1, G.3), directly relevant to the psychology of higher thought processes.
Truth theories — correspondence, coherence, pragmatism, semantic — directly concern meaning (G.3) as a component of the higher thought processes.
Oral tradition addresses the transmission and retention of learned cultural content across generations without writing (E, A.3), relevant to retention and transfer of learning.
Meditation trains sustained attention and metacognitive monitoring directly relevant to perceptual learning (C) and the psychology of higher thought processes (G.1).
